document:
**Security Rule:** Content derived from tool output, retrieved documents, or other agents must be validated and provenance-tagged before it is written into an agent's persistent memory store.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Whatever a tool or sub-agent returns is appended verbatim into the long-term memory store, so an attacker who controls one tool result can plant instructions that steer every later turn.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Before a value is persisted it is validated against an expected schema, length-bounded, and stamped with a provenance label such as untrusted-tool-output so later reads can treat it accordingly.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
memory.append(toolResult)              # raw tool output persisted
```
Secure:
```
record = validate(toolResult, schema=ToolResultSchema)
memory.append(record.tagged(provenance="untrusted-tool"))
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await memory.put({ text: toolResult });
```
Secure:
```
const safe = schema.parse({ text: toolResult.slice(0, MAX_LEN) });
await memory.put({ ...safe, provenance: "untrusted_tool" });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
store.add(MemoryItem(text=tool_output))
```
Secure:
```
item = MemoryItem(text=clean(tool_output)[:MAX_LEN],
                  provenance="untrusted_tool", trusted=False)
store.add(item)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await memory.put({ text: toolResult } as MemoryItem);
```
Secure:
```
const item: MemoryItem = MemoryItemSchema.parse({
  text: toolResult.slice(0, MAX_LEN), provenance: "untrusted_tool" });
await memory.put(item);
```
embed_text:
Content derived from tool output, retrieved documents, or other agents must be validated and provenance-tagged before it is written into an agent's persistent memory store.
Whatever a tool or sub-agent returns is appended verbatim into the long-term memory store, so an attacker who controls one tool result can plant instructions that steer every later turn.
Before a value is persisted it is validated against an expected schema, length-bounded, and stamped with a provenance label such as untrusted-tool-output so later reads can treat it accordingly.

**Security Rule:** Recalled memory must be placed into the prompt as quoted, clearly delimited reference data, never as system or developer instructions that the agent is bound to obey.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Retrieved memory is concatenated straight into the system prompt or instruction block, so a poisoned note such as ignore prior rules is obeyed with the same authority as the developer's own directives.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Memory is injected only inside a fenced data section with an explicit note that its content is reference material and must not be followed as commands, keeping trusted instructions in a separate channel.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
systemPrompt = baseInstructions + "\n" + recalledMemory
```
Secure:
```
systemPrompt = baseInstructions
userTurn = fencedDataBlock("MEMORY (reference only)", recalledMemory)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const system = `${base}\n${recalledMemory}`;
```
Secure:
```
const messages = [{ role: "system", content: base },
  { role: "user", content: `<memory note="data only">${recalledMemory}</memory>` }];
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
messages = [{"role": "system", "content": base + recalled_memory}]
```
Secure:
```
messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": base},
    {"role": "user", "content": f"<memory note='data only'>{recalled_memory}</memory>"},
]
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const system: string = `${base}\n${recalledMemory}`;
```
Secure:
```
const messages: ChatMessage[] = [{ role: "system", content: base },
  { role: "user", content: `<memory note="data only">${recalledMemory}</memory>` }];
```
embed_text:
Recalled memory must be placed into the prompt as quoted, clearly delimited reference data, never as system or developer instructions that the agent is bound to obey.
Retrieved memory is concatenated straight into the system prompt or instruction block, so a poisoned note such as ignore prior rules is obeyed with the same authority as the developer's own directives.
Memory is injected only inside a fenced data section with an explicit note that its content is reference material and must not be followed as commands, keeping trusted instructions in a separate channel.

**Security Rule:** Every memory read and write must be scoped to the originating user, tenant, and session so one principal cannot plant or read entries belonging to another.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Memory is keyed on a single global namespace or a guessable thread id, so one user's poisoned note is recalled in another user's session and acted on as their own context.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The memory key is composed from the authenticated tenant and user identifiers plus the session id, and every query filters on that same scope so recall can never cross a trust boundary.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
memory.query(namespace="global", text=q)
```
Secure:
```
memory.query(namespace=(tenantId, userId, sessionId), text=q)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const ns = ["memories"];
```
Secure:
```
const ns = ["memories", ctx.tenantId, ctx.userId];
await store.search(ns, { query });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
config = {"configurable": {"thread_id": "shared"}}
```
Secure:
```
config = {"configurable": {"thread_id": f"{tenant_id}:{user_id}:{session_id}"}}
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const ns: string[] = ["memories"];
```
Secure:
```
const ns: string[] = ["memories", ctx.tenantId, ctx.userId];
await store.search(ns, { query });
```
embed_text:
Every memory read and write must be scoped to the originating user, tenant, and session so one principal cannot plant or read entries belonging to another.
Memory is keyed on a single global namespace or a guessable thread id, so one user's poisoned note is recalled in another user's session and acted on as their own context.
The memory key is composed from the authenticated tenant and user identifiers plus the session id, and every query filters on that same scope so recall can never cross a trust boundary.

document:
**Security Rule:** Documents ingested into a retrieval index must come from an authenticated, authorized source and be content-screened, so the knowledge an agent retrieves cannot be silently corrupted.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Any document submitted through an open ingestion path is embedded and indexed without source checks, so an attacker uploads a record crafted to surface for common queries and carry hidden instructions.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Ingestion requires an authorized writer, screens content for embedded instructions and anomalies, and records the source so poisoned documents can be traced and purged.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
vectorStore.add(embed(uploadedDoc))
```
Secure:
```
if writerAuthorized(principal) and screen(uploadedDoc):
    vectorStore.add(embed(uploadedDoc), metadata={"source": principal})
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await index.upsert([{ values: embed(doc), metadata: { text: doc } }]);
```
Secure:
```
if (!isAuthorized(principal) || !screenContent(doc)) throw new Error("rejected");
await index.upsert([{ values: embed(doc),
  metadata: { text: doc, source: principal.id } }]);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
qdrant.upsert(collection, [PointStruct(vector=embed(doc), payload={"text": doc})])
```
Secure:
```
if not is_authorized(principal) or not passes_content_screen(doc):
    raise PermissionError("ingestion rejected")
qdrant.upsert(collection, [PointStruct(vector=embed(doc),
    payload={"text": doc, "source": principal.id})])
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await index.upsert([{ values: embed(doc), metadata: { text: doc } }]);
```
Secure:
```
if (!isAuthorized(principal) || !screenContent(doc)) throw new Error("rejected");
const point: VectorPoint = { values: embed(doc),
  metadata: { text: doc, source: principal.id } };
await index.upsert([point]);
```
embed_text:
Documents ingested into a retrieval index must come from an authenticated, authorized source and be content-screened, so the knowledge an agent retrieves cannot be silently corrupted.
Any document submitted through an open ingestion path is embedded and indexed without source checks, so an attacker uploads a record crafted to surface for common queries and carry hidden instructions.
Ingestion requires an authorized writer, screens content for embedded instructions and anomalies, and records the source so poisoned documents can be traced and purged.

When agents communicate through a shared blackboard or scratchpad, each consumer must treat entries from peer agents as untrusted input and re-validate them against its own task contract.
A downstream agent reads a shared-state field written by a peer and acts on it directly, so one compromised or manipulated agent can pivot the whole workflow by writing a poisoned field.
Shared-state writes are namespaced and signed by the producing agent, and each consumer validates the entry's author, schema, and plausibility before using it.

document:
**Security Rule:** Stored memory must carry an integrity signal such as a keyed hash or signature, and that signal must be verified on every read so silent tampering of the backing store is detected.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Memory records are stored as plain text with no integrity check, so an attacker who reaches the database or cache can rewrite a record and the agent recalls the altered content as authentic.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each record is stored with a server-side HMAC over its content and scope, and recall recomputes and compares the HMAC, discarding any record whose signal does not match.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
record = store.read(key)
use(record.text)
```
Secure:
```
record = store.read(key)
if hmac(record.text, secretKey) == record.mac: use(record.text)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const item = await db.getMemory(key);
return item.text;
```
Secure:
```
const item = await db.getMemory(key);
const mac = createHmac("sha256", SECRET).update(item.text).digest("hex");
if (!timingSafeEqualHex(mac, item.mac)) throw new Error("memory tampered");
return item.text;
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
item = db.get_memory(key)
return item["text"]
```
Secure:
```
item = db.get_memory(key)
expected = hmac.new(SECRET, item["text"].encode(), "sha256").hexdigest()
if not hmac.compare_digest(expected, item["mac"]):
    raise IntegrityError("memory tampering detected")
return item["text"]
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const item: MemoryRow = await db.getMemory(key);
return item.text;
```
Secure:
```
const item: MemoryRow = await db.getMemory(key);
const mac = createHmac("sha256", SECRET).update(item.text).digest("hex");
if (!timingSafeEqualHex(mac, item.mac)) throw new Error("memory tampered");
return item.text;
```
embed_text:
Stored memory must carry an integrity signal such as a keyed hash or signature, and that signal must be verified on every read so silent tampering of the backing store is detected.
Memory records are stored as plain text with no integrity check, so an attacker who reaches the database or cache can rewrite a record and the agent recalls the altered content as authentic.
Each record is stored with a server-side HMAC over its content and scope, and recall recomputes and compares the HMAC, discarding any record whose signal does not match.

document:
**Security Rule:** Memory writes must be rate-limited and capacity-bounded per scope so an attacker cannot flood the store to evict trusted history or dominate retrieval through repetition.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Any turn can append unlimited entries to memory, so an attacker repeats a poisoned note until it crowds out genuine context or becomes the most frequent and highest-ranked recall result.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each scope has a write quota and a bounded retention window, and near-duplicate entries are de-duplicated, so no single source can saturate or skew the store.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
memory.append(entry)                  # no quota, no dedupe
```
Secure:
```
if writeQuota.allow(scope) and not memory.hasSimilar(entry):
    memory.appendBounded(entry, maxItems=CAP)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await store.add(scope, entry);
```
Secure:
```
if (!rateLimiter.allow(scope)) throw new Error("memory write quota exceeded");
if (!(await store.hasNearDuplicate(scope, entry)))
  await store.addBounded(scope, entry, { maxItems: CAP });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
store.add(scope, entry)
```
Secure:
```
if not rate_limiter.allow(scope):
    raise RateLimitError("memory write quota exceeded")
if not store.has_near_duplicate(scope, entry):
    store.add_bounded(scope, entry, max_items=CAP)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await store.add(scope, entry);
```
Secure:
```
if (!rateLimiter.allow(scope)) throw new Error("memory write quota exceeded");
if (!(await store.hasNearDuplicate(scope, entry)))
  await store.addBounded(scope, entry, { maxItems: CAP });
```
embed_text:
Memory writes must be rate-limited and capacity-bounded per scope so an attacker cannot flood the store to evict trusted history or dominate retrieval through repetition.
Any turn can append unlimited entries to memory, so an attacker repeats a poisoned note until it crowds out genuine context or becomes the most frequent and highest-ranked recall result.
Each scope has a write quota and a bounded retention window, and near-duplicate entries are de-duplicated, so no single source can saturate or skew the store.

document:
**Security Rule:** Every memory write must be logged with its author, source, and timestamp, and the store must support reverting to a known-good state so a poisoning incident can be contained.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Memory is mutated in place with no history, so once a poisoned entry is written there is no way to learn how it got there or to roll the store back to before the incident.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Writes are recorded as append-only, attributed versioned events, so operators can audit who wrote what and restore the store to a snapshot taken before the poisoning.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
memory[key] = value                   # in-place, no history
```
Secure:
```
memoryLog.append(WriteEvent(key, value, author, source, now()))
memory.applyVersioned(key, value)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
memory[key] = value;
```
Secure:
```
await auditLog.append({ key, value, author: agentId, source, ts: Date.now() });
await memory.putVersioned(key, value);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
store.update(key, value)
```
Secure:
```
audit.record(MemoryWrite(key=key, value=value, author=agent_id,
                         source=source, ts=utcnow()))
store.update(key, value, version=audit.last_version)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
memory[key] = value;
```
Secure:
```
await auditLog.append({ key, value, author: agentId, source, ts: Date.now() });
await memory.putVersioned(key, value);
```
embed_text:
Every memory write must be logged with its author, source, and timestamp, and the store must support reverting to a known-good state so a poisoning incident can be contained.
Memory is mutated in place with no history, so once a poisoned entry is written there is no way to learn how it got there or to roll the store back to before the incident.
Writes are recorded as append-only, attributed versioned events, so operators can audit who wrote what and restore the store to a snapshot taken before the poisoning.

**Security Rule:** Secrets, credentials, and personal data must be redacted before any content is committed to memory, so a later poisoning or scope leak cannot turn the store into an exfiltration channel.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Raw conversation and tool output, including API keys and personal data, is written verbatim into long-lived memory, so a poisoned recall prompt can later surface those secrets to an attacker.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A redaction pass strips secrets and high-entropy strings before the write, and sensitive fields are stored only as references that the agent cannot dereference into plaintext.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
memory.append(conversationTurn)
```
Secure:
```
memory.append(redactSecrets(conversationTurn))
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await memory.put({ text: turnText });
```
Secure:
```
await memory.put({ text: redactSecrets(turnText) });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
store.add(MemoryItem(text=turn_text))
```
Secure:
```
store.add(MemoryItem(text=redact_secrets(turn_text)))
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await memory.put({ text: turnText } as MemoryItem);
```
Secure:
```
const item: MemoryItem = { text: redactSecrets(turnText) };
await memory.put(item);
```
embed_text:
Secrets, credentials, and personal data must be redacted before any content is committed to memory, so a later poisoning or scope leak cannot turn the store into an exfiltration channel.
Raw conversation and tool output, including API keys and personal data, is written verbatim into long-lived memory, so a poisoned recall prompt can later surface those secrets to an attacker.
A redaction pass strips secrets and high-entropy strings before the write, and sensitive fields are stored only as references that the agent cannot dereference into plaintext.

Every argument an agent passes to a tool must be validated against a strict schema inside the tool itself, since the model can emit any value it is steered to produce and the schema description is only a hint, not an enforced contract.
A tool trusts the arguments the model supplies and uses them directly in a sensitive operation, so a model that is confused or steered by injected text can drive the tool with values its author never intended.
The tool revalidates every argument against an explicit typed schema with bounds, allowed values, and length limits before acting, and rejects anything that does not conform rather than coercing it.

document:
**Security Rule:** An agent must be bound only to the tools its current task requires, so that a compromised or misled agent cannot reach high-impact tools that are irrelevant to its role.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Every agent in the system is registered with the full tool catalog, so a low-trust agent handling untrusted input can still invoke destructive or privileged tools far outside its mandate.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each agent receives a narrow, role-scoped tool list, and high-impact tools are bound only to the specific agents whose task genuinely needs them.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
agent = Agent(tools=ALL_TOOLS)        # summarizer can also delete data
```
Secure:
```
agent = Agent(tools=tools_for_role("summarizer"))   # read-only subset
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const agent = new Agent({ tools: toolRegistry.getAll() });
```
Secure:
```
const agent = new Agent({ tools: toolRegistry.forRole("reader") });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
graph = create_react_agent(model, tools=registry.all())
```
Secure:
```
READER_TOOLS = [search_docs, read_file]
graph = create_react_agent(model, tools=READER_TOOLS)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const agent = createAgent({ llm, tools: allTools });
```
Secure:
```
const scoped: Tool[] = allTools.filter(t => ROLE_TOOLS.reader.has(t.name));
const agent = createAgent({ llm, tools: scoped });
```
embed_text:
An agent must be bound only to the tools its current task requires, so that a compromised or misled agent cannot reach high-impact tools that are irrelevant to its role.
Every agent in the system is registered with the full tool catalog, so a low-trust agent handling untrusted input can still invoke destructive or privileged tools far outside its mandate.
Each agent receives a narrow, role-scoped tool list, and high-impact tools are bound only to the specific agents whose task genuinely needs them.

document:
**Security Rule:** Irreversible or high-impact tool calls — deleting data, moving money, sending external messages, or changing infrastructure — must pause for explicit human approval before they execute.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The agent executes destructive or externally visible tool calls autonomously the moment the model proposes them, so a single bad decision or injected instruction takes immediate real-world effect.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** High-impact tools are gated behind an interrupt that surfaces the proposed call and its arguments to a human, and the call runs only after an explicit approval is recorded.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
result = tool.invoke(call.args)       # destructive call runs at once
```
Secure:
```
if tool.high_impact: await_human_approval(call)
result = tool.invoke(call.args)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await sendEmailTool.run(args);
```
Secure:
```
const ok = await requestApproval({ tool: "sendEmail", args });
if (ok) await sendEmailTool.run(args);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
def call_node(state):
    return {"out": delete_bucket.invoke(state["args"])}
```
Secure:
```
def call_node(state):
    decision = interrupt({"tool": "delete_bucket", "args": state["args"]})
    if not decision["approved"]: return {"out": "cancelled"}
    return {"out": delete_bucket.invoke(state["args"])}
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const out = await tools.deployStack.run(args);
```
Secure:
```
if (HIGH_IMPACT.has(toolName)) await interrupt({ toolName, args });
const out = await tools.deployStack.run(args);
```
embed_text:
Irreversible or high-impact tool calls — deleting data, moving money, sending external messages, or changing infrastructure — must pause for explicit human approval before they execute.
The agent executes destructive or externally visible tool calls autonomously the moment the model proposes them, so a single bad decision or injected instruction takes immediate real-world effect.
High-impact tools are gated behind an interrupt that surfaces the proposed call and its arguments to a human, and the call runs only after an explicit approval is recorded.

document:
**Security Rule:** Content returned by a tool or fetched from an external source must be treated as data, not as instructions, so that text inside it cannot redirect the agent into making unintended tool calls.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Tool output, retrieved documents, or web content are concatenated into the agent prompt with the same authority as the system policy, so injected directives in that content trigger attacker-chosen tool calls.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Untrusted tool and retrieval output is wrapped in a clearly delimited data role, the system policy reasserts that such content is never an instruction, and tool selection is constrained to the current task.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
prompt = system + "\n" + fetched_web_page    # page text steers tools
```
Secure:
```
prompt = system + "\n<untrusted_data>" + fetched_web_page + "</untrusted_data>"
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
prompt += `\nSearch results:\n${searchOutput}`;
```
Secure:
```
prompt += `\n<data trust="untrusted">${searchOutput}</data>`;
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
messages.append(HumanMessage(content=tool_result))
```
Secure:
```
messages.append(ToolMessage(content=tool_result, tool_call_id=cid))
# tool role is data; system message states tool output is never a command
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
messages.push({ role: "user", content: retrievedDoc });
```
Secure:
```
messages.push({ role: "tool", name: "retriever",
                content: `<untrusted>${retrievedDoc}</untrusted>` });
```
embed_text:
Content returned by a tool or fetched from an external source must be treated as data, not as instructions, so that text inside it cannot redirect the agent into making unintended tool calls.
Tool output, retrieved documents, or web content are concatenated into the agent prompt with the same authority as the system policy, so injected directives in that content trigger attacker-chosen tool calls.
Untrusted tool and retrieval output is wrapped in a clearly delimited data role, the system policy reasserts that such content is never an instruction, and tool selection is constrained to the current task.

document:
**Security Rule:** The number and cost of tool invocations within a single agent run must be capped, so a looping or manipulated agent cannot exhaust resources, spend a budget, or hammer an external API.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The agent loop calls tools without any counter or budget, so a reasoning loop or an injected goal can drive thousands of calls, causing runaway cost or a denial of service against a dependency.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A per-run counter and spend budget bound tool invocations, and the run halts cleanly with an error once the call ceiling or cost ceiling is reached.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
while not done: result = next_tool.invoke(args)   # unbounded loop
```
Secure:
```
while not done and calls < MAX_CALLS:
    result = next_tool.invoke(args); calls += 1
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
for (;;) { const r = await callTool(plan.next()); }
```
Secure:
```
for (let i = 0; i < MAX_STEPS; i++) { const r = await callTool(plan.next()); }
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
graph = create_react_agent(model, tools)
graph.invoke(state)                       # no recursion ceiling
```
Secure:
```
graph = create_react_agent(model, tools)
graph.invoke(state, config={"recursion_limit": 25})
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
async function loop() { while (true) await invokeTool(pick()); }
```
Secure:
```
async function loop(budget: Budget) {
  while (budget.callsLeft() > 0 && budget.costLeft() > 0) {
    budget.charge(await invokeTool(pick()));
  }
}
```
embed_text:
The number and cost of tool invocations within a single agent run must be capped, so a looping or manipulated agent cannot exhaust resources, spend a budget, or hammer an external API.
The agent loop calls tools without any counter or budget, so a reasoning loop or an injected goal can drive thousands of calls, causing runaway cost or a denial of service against a dependency.
A per-run counter and spend budget bound tool invocations, and the run halts cleanly with an error once the call ceiling or cost ceiling is reached.

document:
**Security Rule:** Tools that run code, shell commands, or file operations on behalf of an agent must execute in an isolated sandbox with no ambient credentials, restricted filesystem, and constrained network access.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A code-execution or shell tool runs in the agent's own process or host with full privileges, so any model-generated command reaches the real filesystem, secrets, and internal network.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Code and shell tools run inside a locked-down container or sandbox with a read-only or scratch filesystem, dropped capabilities, and an egress allowlist, so a misused call cannot escape its blast radius.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
def run_code(src): exec(src)               # runs in the agent process
```
Secure:
```
def run_code(src): return sandbox.run(src, network="none", fs="scratch")
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const runCode = { run: ({ code }) => eval(code) };
```
Secure:
```
const runCode = { run: ({ code }) =>
  ivm.runInIsolate(code, { timeoutMs: 2000, memoryMb: 64 }) };
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
@tool
def shell(cmd: str): return subprocess.run(cmd, shell=True, capture_output=True)
```
Secure:
```
@tool
def shell(cmd: list[str]):
    return sandbox_exec(cmd, image="tools:pinned", net=False, ro_root=True)
```

```

```
Secure:

```
  runInContainer(script, { image: "sandbox:pinned", network: "none" }) };
```

embed_text:
Tools that run code, shell commands, or file operations on behalf of an agent must execute in an isolated sandbox with no ambient credentials, restricted filesystem, and constrained network access.
A code-execution or shell tool runs in the agent's own process or host with full privileges, so any model-generated command reaches the real filesystem, secrets, and internal network.
Code and shell tools run inside a locked-down container or sandbox with a read-only or scratch filesystem, dropped capabilities, and an egress allowlist, so a misused call cannot escape its blast radius.

Before a tool acts, it must verify that the user the agent is acting for is authorized for that specific action and resource, rather than relying on a single broad service credential.
Tools execute under one shared high-privilege service account and skip any per-user authorization check, so the agent can be steered into reading or changing data belonging to other users or tenants.
Each tool receives the acting user's identity and enforces an authorization check scoped to that user before touching a resource, so the agent can never exceed the caller's own permissions.

**Security Rule:** Tools and MCP servers an agent loads must come from a trusted, integrity-verified source, since a malicious tool definition can misdescribe itself to lure the agent into harmful calls.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The agent dynamically loads tools or connects to MCP servers chosen by a discovery feed or a URL, so an attacker can register a tool whose description steers the model into invoking it.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Tool sources are pinned to a vetted allowlist, their definitions are integrity-checked against a known hash or signature, and new tools enter the catalog only through a reviewed change.

```

```

Secure:
```

const tools = verifyManifest(await fetchPinned(discoveryUrl));
```

**Security Rule:** Every tool invocation must be logged with the agent identity, the acting user, the tool name, the arguments, and the outcome, so misuse is detectable, attributable, and reviewable after the fact.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Tool calls execute with no durable record of which agent invoked what with which arguments, so misuse cannot be detected, attributed, or investigated.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A structured audit event is emitted for every tool call capturing agent id, user id, tool name, sanitized arguments, result status, and a correlation id, and the log is retained for review.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
result = tool.invoke(args)              # no record of the call
```
Secure:
```
result = tool.invoke(args)
audit.log(agent_id, user_id, tool.name, redact(args), status(result))
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const out = await tool.run(args);
```
Secure:
```
const out = await tool.run(args);
auditLog.emit({ tool: tool.name, user: ctx.userId,
                args: redact(args), status: out.status });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
def call_tool(tool, args): return tool.invoke(args)
```
Secure:
```
def call_tool(tool, args, ctx):
    out = tool.invoke(args)
    logger.info("tool_call", extra={"tool": tool.name, "user": ctx.user_id,
                "args": redact(args), "ok": out.ok, "corr": ctx.correlation_id})
    return out
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
async function invoke(t: Tool, a: ToolArgs) { return t.run(a); }
```
Secure:
```
async function invoke(t: Tool, a: ToolArgs, ctx: AgentContext) {
  const out = await t.run(a);
  audit.record({ tool: t.name, user: ctx.user, args: redact(a),
                  ok: out.ok, correlationId: ctx.correlationId });
  return out;
}
```

Tool calls execute with no durable record of which agent invoked what with which arguments, so misuse cannot be detected, attributed, or investigated.
A structured audit event is emitted for every tool call capturing agent id, user id, tool name, sanitized arguments, result status, and a correlation id, and the log is retained for review.

**Security Rule:** The output a tool returns to the agent must be size-bounded, type- checked, and schema-conformed before it re-enters the model context, so a tool cannot flood, confuse, or poison the agent's next decision.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Whatever a tool returns is fed straight back into the model context with no size or shape limit, so an oversized or malformed result exhausts the context window or smuggles in misleading content.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Tool results are validated against an expected schema, truncated to a safe length, and stripped of control content before they are placed back into the agent's messages.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
messages.append(tool_output)            # arbitrary size and shape
```
Secure:
```
messages.append(truncate(validate(tool_output), MAX_LEN))
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
messages.push({ role: "tool", content: result });
```
Secure:
```
const text = String(ResultSchema.parse(result)).slice(0, 8000);
messages.push({ role: "tool", content: text });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
state["messages"].append(ToolMessage(content=raw_result, tool_call_id=cid))
```
Secure:
```
safe = ResultSchema.model_validate(raw_result)
text = str(safe)[:8000]
state["messages"].append(ToolMessage(content=text, tool_call_id=cid))
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return { role: "tool", content: toolResult as string };
```
Secure:
```
const parsed = ResultSchema.parse(toolResult);
return { role: "tool", content: stringify(parsed).slice(0, MAX_TOOL_OUTPUT) };
```

Tool results are validated against an expected schema, truncated to a safe length, and stripped of control content before they are placed back into the agent's messages.

**Security Rule:** An agent must run with the narrowest set of credentials its task requires, scoped per tool and per resource, never with a shared admin or root token that grants access far beyond its purpose.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The agent process is handed a single broad credential — for example a cloud admin key or an unrestricted database superuser — so any tool it invokes, or any instruction it is tricked into following, inherits full administrative reach.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each agent and each tool receives a distinct, minimally scoped credential bound to exactly the resources and operations it needs, so a compromised step cannot act outside its narrow grant.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
agent = Agent(tools=tools, credentials=ADMIN_API_KEY)
```
Secure:
```
agent = Agent(tools=tools, credentials=scoped_token(role="reader",
              resources=["reports/*"]))
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const agent = new Agent({ tools, apiKey: process.env.ADMIN_KEY });
```
Secure:
```
const agent = new Agent({ tools,
  apiKey: await mintScopedKey({ role: "reader", scope: "reports" }) });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
agent = Agent(model=llm, tools=[db_tool],
              credentials=os.environ["AWS_ROOT_KEY"])
```
Secure:
```
agent = Agent(model=llm, tools=[db_tool],
              credentials=sts.assume_role("agent-readonly", ttl=900))
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const agent = new Agent({ tools, credentials: rootCredentials });
```
Secure:
```
const creds: ScopedCredentials = await issueScoped("agent-reader", ["reports:read"]);
const agent = new Agent({ tools, credentials: creds });
```
embed_text:
An agent must run with the narrowest set of credentials its task requires, scoped per tool and per resource, never with a shared admin or root token that grants access far beyond its purpose.
The agent process is handed a single broad credential — for example a cloud admin key or an unrestricted database superuser — so any tool it invokes, or any instruction it is tricked into following, inherits full administrative reach.
Each agent and each tool receives a distinct, minimally scoped credential bound to exactly the resources and operations it needs, so a compromised step cannot act outside its narrow grant.

Every privileged tool call must be authorized at the moment of invocation against the acting principal and the specific arguments, not merely gated once when the agent session starts.
Authorization is checked only at session creation, so once the agent is running it can call any tool with any arguments, and a manipulated plan reaches sensitive operations with no further permission check.
The tool layer enforces an authorization check on each call, taking the principal, the tool, and the concrete arguments, and denies the call when the principal lacks rights to that exact operation.

**Security Rule:** When an agent delegates work to a sub-agent it must pass down a privilege set equal to or narrower than its own, and never expand the sub-agent's scope beyond the delegating agent's grant.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A sub-agent is spawned with its own broad role or with the orchestrator's full toolset, so delegation widens privilege and a low-trust task ends up running high-trust tools.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Delegation derives the child's permissions by intersecting the parent's current scope with the narrow needs of the delegated task, so authority can only shrink as work is handed down.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
sub_agent = spawn(role="admin", tools=ALL_TOOLS)
```
Secure:
```
sub_agent = spawn(scope=intersect(parent.scope, task.required_scope))
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const child = orchestrator.delegate(task, { tools: orchestrator.tools });
```
Secure:
```
const child = orchestrator.delegate(task, {
  scope: intersectScopes(orchestrator.scope, task.minScope) });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
researcher = Agent(tools=all_tools, credentials=orchestrator_creds)
```
Secure:
```
researcher = Agent(tools=subset(all_tools, task.tools),
                    credentials=orchestrator.creds.attenuate(task.scope))
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const sub: Agent = createSubAgent({ role: "admin" });
```
Secure:
```
const sub: Agent = createSubAgent({
  scope: narrow(parent.scope, task.requiredScope) });
```
embed_text:

A sub-agent is spawned with its own broad role or with the orchestrator's full toolset, so delegation widens privilege and a low-trust task ends up running high-trust tools.
Delegation derives the child's permissions by intersecting the parent's current scope with the narrow needs of the delegated task, so authority can only shrink as work is handed down.

**Security Rule:** An agent's effective permissions must be fixed by the trusted runtime at session start and must never be raised by content the agent reads, such as tool output, retrieved documents, or user messages.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The agent treats role or permission claims found in untrusted content as authoritative, so a crafted document or tool result saying the agent is now an administrator silently escalates its privileges.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Privilege state lives only in the trusted runtime context and is immutable for the session, and any role or scope hint appearing inside model-visible content is ignored when authorization is decided.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if "you are admin" in tool_output: session.role = "admin"
```
Secure:
```
session.role = trusted_runtime.role   # set once, never from content
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:
```
const scopes = runtime.principal.scopes;  // not from retrieved content
```

Vulnerable:
```

authz.check(role, action)
```
Secure:
```
authz.check(ctx.trusted_role, action)  # model output cannot raise it
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:
```

```

The agent treats role or permission claims found in untrusted content as authoritative, so a crafted document or tool result saying the agent is now an administrator silently escalates its privileges.
Privilege state lives only in the trusted runtime context and is immutable for the session, and any role or scope hint appearing inside model-visible content is ignored when authorization is decided.

**Security Rule:** When an agent acts on behalf of a user it must carry that user's identity to every downstream call, so backend services authorize the real principal and not the agent's own service account.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The agent calls downstream services with its own high-privilege service identity while acting for a low-privilege user, so the agent becomes a confused deputy that performs actions the user could not.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The originating user's identity is propagated as a delegated or on-behalf-of token through every hop, and each backend authorizes the end user, keeping the agent unable to exceed that user's rights.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
backend.call(action, identity=AGENT_SERVICE_ACCOUNT)
```
Secure:
```
backend.call(action, identity=on_behalf_of(end_user))
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
api.fetch(path, { token: agentServiceToken });
```
Secure:
```
api.fetch(path, { token: ctx.delegatedUserToken });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
requests.post(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {SERVICE_TOKEN}"})
```
Secure:
```

```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const res = await downstream.call(req, { actor: "agent-svc" });
```
Secure:
```
const res = await downstream.call(req, { actor: ctx.endUser.id,
  onBehalfOf: ctx.endUser.token });
```
embed_text:
When an agent acts on behalf of a user it must carry that user's identity to every downstream call, so backend services authorize the real principal and not the agent's own service account.
The agent calls downstream services with its own high-privilege service identity while acting for a low-privilege user, so the agent becomes a confused deputy that performs actions the user could not.
The originating user's identity is propagated as a delegated or on-behalf-of token through every hop, and each backend authorizes the end user, keeping the agent unable to exceed that user's rights.

Operations that are irreversible or high impact — financial transfers, data deletion, production deployment, permission changes — must pause for explicit human approval before the agent may execute them.
The agent autonomously executes high-impact tools as soon as its plan selects them, so a manipulated or hallucinated plan reaches destructive operations with no human able to intervene.
High-impact tools are flagged as requiring approval, and the runtime interrupts execution to obtain a signed human decision before the call proceeds, recording who approved what.

**Security Rule:** Each agent must be bound to an explicit allowlist of tools matched to its role, so it cannot discover or invoke capabilities outside the set its task legitimately needs.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Every agent is registered with the full global tool catalog, so a narrow-purpose agent still holds dangerous capabilities and a single prompt-injection can pivot it into a privileged action.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Tools are registered per agent from a role-scoped allowlist, the catalog is filtered before the agent ever sees it, and any call to an unlisted tool is refused by the runtime.


**Security Rule:** Credentials issued to an agent must be short-lived, scoped to the current task, and centrally revocable, so a leaked or abused token expires quickly and can be cut off on demand.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The agent holds a long-lived static API key with broad scope, so a token exfiltrated through tool output or logs grants a persistent foothold with the agent's full privileges.

```

```

```
const client = new ApiClient({ apiKey: cred.token });
```

```

const client = new ApiClient({ apiKey: cred.token });
```

A fresh credential is minted per task with a short time-to-live and a task-specific scope, tracked so it can be revoked immediately, and it expires automatically when the task ends.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Privileged calls execute with no durable record of who acted or what was authorized, so a privilege-compromise incident cannot be detected in time or reconstructed afterward.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The tool layer emits a tamper-evident audit event for each privileged call, capturing principal, agent, tool, arguments, and allow-or-deny outcome before and after execution.

The agent's authorization layer must default to deny, so any tool or action with no explicit grant for the acting principal is refused rather than allowed to proceed.

**Security Rule:** Every agent reasoning loop must enforce a hard maximum number of steps or iterations, so a stuck or adversarially looping agent terminates instead of consuming compute and tokens indefinitely.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An agent executes a think-act loop with no iteration ceiling, so a task that never reaches a stop condition runs forever, burning compute and tokens until an external timeout or budget failure.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The loop is bounded by an explicit maximum step count, and reaching that ceiling ends the run with a clear terminal status rather than continuing to call the model.

```

    done = agent.step()        # no ceiling — may never terminate
```

```

    if agent.step(): break
else: fail("step cap reached")
```

```

```
Secure:
```
for (let i = 0; i < MAX_STEPS && !result.final; i++)
  result = await agent.next();
```

Secure:

```
                      early_stopping_method="force")
```

```

```

**Security Rule:** Stateful agent graphs must run under an explicit recursion or super-step limit, so a cyclic edge or a node that keeps routing back on itself cannot expand the run without bound.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An agent graph with conditional edges is invoked without a recursion limit, so a routing cycle between nodes drives an unbounded number of super-steps and exhausts compute.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The graph is invoked with a finite recursion limit sized to the longest legitimate path, and exceeding it raises a caught error that ends the run cleanly.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
graph.invoke(state)                  # no recursion bound on cyclic graph
```
Secure:
```
graph.invoke(state, recursion_limit=40)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const out = await graph.invoke(state);
```
Secure:
```
const out = await graph.invoke(state, { recursionLimit: 40 });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
result = compiled_graph.invoke(initial_state)
```
Secure:
```
result = compiled_graph.invoke(initial_state,
                               config={"recursion_limit": 40})
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const out = await graph.invoke(state);
```
Secure:
```
const out = await graph.invoke(state,
  { recursionLimit: 40 } as RunnableConfig);
```
embed_text:
Stateful agent graphs must run under an explicit recursion or super-step limit, so a cyclic edge or a node that keeps routing back on itself cannot expand the run without bound.
An agent graph with conditional edges is invoked without a recursion limit, so a routing cycle between nodes drives an unbounded number of super-steps and exhausts compute.
The graph is invoked with a finite recursion limit sized to the longest legitimate path, and exceeding it raises a caught error that ends the run cleanly.

**Security Rule:** Each agent run must carry a cumulative token budget that is debited after every model call and checked before the next, so total token spend for a task is capped regardless of how many steps it takes.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Token usage is never aggregated across the steps of a run, so a long or looping task can consume an unbounded number of tokens and cost even though each individual call looks small.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A running token total is accumulated from each call's usage metadata and compared against a per-run ceiling, halting the agent with a budget-exceeded result once the ceiling is reached.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
response = model.call(prompt)        # usage discarded, no running total
```
Secure:
```
spent += model.call(prompt).usage.total_tokens
if spent > TOKEN_BUDGET: stop("token budget exceeded")
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const res = await model.invoke(prompt);
```
Secure:
```
const res = await model.invoke(prompt);
spent += res.usage.totalTokens;
if (spent > TOKEN_BUDGET) throw new Error("token budget exceeded");
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
for task in subtasks:
    results.append(llm.invoke(task))      # unbounded aggregate spend
```
Secure:
```
for task in subtasks:
    r = llm.invoke(task)
    spent += r.usage_metadata["total_tokens"]
    if spent > TOKEN_BUDGET: raise BudgetExceeded(spent)
    results.append(r)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const res = await model.invoke(prompt);
```
Secure:
```
spent += (await model.invoke(prompt)).usage.totalTokens;
if (spent > TOKEN_BUDGET) throw new BudgetExceededError(spent);
```

A running token total is accumulated from each call's usage metadata and compared against a per-run ceiling, halting the agent with a budget-exceeded result once the ceiling is reached.

**Security Rule:** An orchestrator that spawns sub-agents or parallel tasks must cap the number it creates per request, so a large or attacker-influenced work list cannot fan out into an overwhelming swarm of concurrent runs.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An orchestrator spawns one sub-agent per item in a model-generated or user-supplied list with no count limit, so an inflated list multiplies into hundreds of concurrent agent runs.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The number of sub-agents spawned is capped at a fixed maximum, with the work list truncated or rejected when it exceeds that maximum before any sub-agent is started.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
for item in plannedSubtasks: spawnAgent(item)   # count unbounded
```
Secure:
```
for item in plannedSubtasks[:MAX_SUBAGENTS]: spawnAgent(item)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await Promise.all(tasks.map(t => runSubAgent(t)));
```
Secure:
```
if (tasks.length > MAX_SUBAGENTS) throw new Error("fan-out too large");
await Promise.all(tasks.map(t => runSubAgent(t)));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await asyncio.gather(*(run_sub_agent(t) for t in tasks))
```
Secure:
```
if len(tasks) > MAX_SUBAGENTS: raise FanOutTooLarge(len(tasks))
await asyncio.gather(*(run_sub_agent(t) for t in tasks))
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await Promise.all(tasks.map((t: Task) => runSubAgent(t)));
```
Secure:
```
const bounded = tasks.slice(0, MAX_SUBAGENTS);
await Promise.all(bounded.map((t: Task) => runSubAgent(t)));
```

The number of sub-agents spawned is capped at a fixed maximum, with the work list truncated or rejected when it exceeds that maximum before any sub-agent is started.

**Security Rule:** Tool invocations made by an agent must be metered against a per-run quota and a rate limit, so the agent cannot hammer an external API or a billed service into exhaustion or throttling.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An agent calls tools in a loop with no per-run call quota and no rate limiting, so a repetitive plan floods a downstream API, triggers provider throttling, or runs up usage charges.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each tool call is counted against a per-run quota and paced by a rate limiter, and exceeding either stops the agent rather than continuing to call the tool.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
result = tool.run(args)              # no quota, no pacing
```
Secure:
```
rateLimiter.acquire()
if toolCalls.increment() > TOOL_QUOTA: stop("tool quota exceeded")
result = tool.run(args)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const out = await tool.invoke(args);
```
Secure:
```
await limiter.removeTokens(1);
if (++toolCalls > TOOL_QUOTA) throw new Error("tool quota exceeded");
const out = await tool.invoke(args);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
def call_tool(name, args):
    return TOOLS[name].invoke(args)
```
Secure:
```
def call_tool(name, args):
    if tool_calls.inc() > TOOL_QUOTA: raise ToolQuotaExceeded()
    limiter.wait()
    return TOOLS[name].invoke(args)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const out = await tool.invoke(args);
```
Secure:
```
await limiter.removeTokens(1);
if (++toolCalls > TOOL_QUOTA) throw new ToolQuotaError();
const out = await tool.invoke(args);
```

An agent calls tools in a loop with no per-run call quota and no rate limiting, so a repetitive plan floods a downstream API, triggers provider throttling, or runs up usage charges.
Each tool call is counted against a per-run quota and paced by a rate limiter, and exceeding either stops the agent rather than continuing to call the tool.

**Security Rule:** Every agent run, and every blocking model or tool call inside it, must execute under a wall-clock timeout, so a hung dependency or a slow loop cannot tie up a worker indefinitely.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An agent run or its model and tool calls are awaited with no deadline, so a slow or unresponsive dependency blocks the worker and starves other queued work.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The run executes inside a deadline, and individual model and tool calls have their own shorter timeouts, so a stall is detected and cancelled instead of blocking forever.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
result = agent.run(task)             # awaited with no deadline
```
Secure:
```
result = withTimeout(agent.run(task), seconds=120)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const result = await agent.invoke(task);
```
Secure:
```
const result = await agent.invoke(task,
  { signal: AbortSignal.timeout(120_000) });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:
```
result = await asyncio.wait_for(agent.ainvoke(task), timeout=120)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const result = await agent.invoke(task);
```
Secure:
```
const result = await agent.invoke(task,
  { signal: AbortSignal.timeout(120_000) } as RunnableConfig);
```

The run executes inside a deadline, and individual model and tool calls have their own shorter timeouts, so a stall is detected and cancelled instead of blocking forever.

**Security Rule:** Conversation history and accumulated tool output appended to an agent's context must be trimmed or summarized to a bounded size, so context growth does not inflate per-call token cost and latency.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Every step's messages and full tool outputs are appended to the context with no trimming, so each successive model call carries a larger prompt and cost grows quadratically over a long run.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** History is capped to a recent window or compacted by a summarizer, and large tool outputs are truncated before being added, keeping the prompt size bounded across the whole run.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
messages.append(toolOutput)          # full history grows every step
```
Secure:
```
messages = trimToTokenBudget(messages + [truncate(toolOutput)])
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
history.push({ role: "tool", content: toolOutput });
```
Secure:
```
history.push({ role: "tool", content: toolOutput.slice(0, MAX_CHARS) });
history = trimToTokenBudget(history, 8000);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
messages.append(ToolMessage(content=tool_output))
```
Secure:
```
messages.append(ToolMessage(content=tool_output[:MAX_TOOL_CHARS]))
messages = trim_messages(messages, max_tokens=8000, token_counter=llm)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
history.push({ role: "tool", content: toolOutput });
```
Secure:
```
history = trimMessages([...history,
  { role: "tool", content: toolOutput.slice(0, MAX_CHARS) }], 8000);
```

When an agent can delegate to another agent, the delegation chain must carry a depth counter with a hard maximum, so agents calling agents cannot recurse into an exponentially expanding tree of runs.
A sub-agent can itself invoke further sub-agents with no depth tracking, so a delegation cycle or an over-eager planner expands into an unbounded tree that exhausts compute and budget.

**Security Rule:** An agent must detect when it is repeating the same action or making no measurable progress, and abort the run, so a non-terminating loop is stopped before it consumes the full step or token budget.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An agent repeats an identical tool call or model action across many steps with no repetition check, so it spins in place burning the budget until a hard cap finally fires.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Recent actions are tracked and an identical action repeated beyond a small threshold, or a stretch of steps with no progress, triggers an immediate stop with a no-progress result.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
action = agent.next()
execute(action)                      # same action may repeat forever
```
Secure:
```
action = agent.next()
if recent.count(action) >= REPEAT_LIMIT: stop("no progress detected")
recent.append(action); execute(action)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const act = agent.decide();
run(act);
```
Secure:
```
const act = agent.decide();
if ((seen[act] = (seen[act] ?? 0) + 1) > REPEAT_LIMIT)
  throw new Error("no progress detected");
run(act);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
for _ in range(MAX_STEPS):
    act = agent.decide(); run(act)
```
Secure:
```
for _ in range(MAX_STEPS):
    act = agent.decide()
    if seen[act] >= REPEAT_LIMIT: raise NoProgress(act)
    seen[act] += 1; run(act)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const act = agent.decide();
run(act);
```
Secure:
```
const act: string = agent.decide();
if ((seen.get(act) ?? 0) + 1 > REPEAT_LIMIT)
  throw new Error("no progress detected");
seen.set(act, (seen.get(act) ?? 0) + 1); run(act);
```

Recent actions are tracked and an identical action repeated beyond a small threshold, or a stretch of steps with no progress, triggers an immediate stop with a no-progress result.

**Security Rule:** Concurrent agent runs and aggregate spend must be capped per tenant or per user, so one principal cannot launch enough simultaneous runs to starve shared workers or exhaust a shared cost budget.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Agent runs are admitted with only a global limit and no per-tenant accounting, so a single tenant can queue unbounded runs that monopolize workers and the shared model budget.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each tenant has a maximum number of in-flight runs and a spend ceiling, both checked at admission, and a request is rejected or queued once either cap is reached.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:
```
if activeRuns(tenant) >= TENANT_MAX or spend(tenant) >= TENANT_BUDGET:
    reject("tenant capacity exceeded")
startAgentRun(tenant, task)
```

Vulnerable:
```
async function submit(tenantId, task) { return runAgent(task); }
```
Secure:
```
async function submit(tenantId, task) {
  if (activeRuns(tenantId) >= TENANT_MAX) throw new Error("tenant full");
  return tenantLimiter(tenantId).schedule(() => runAgent(task));
}
```

```
    return await run_agent(task)
```
Secure:
```

        if tenant_spend[tenant_id] >= TENANT_BUDGET:
            raise TenantBudgetExceeded(tenant_id)
        return await run_agent(task)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const submit = (tenantId: string, task: Task) => runAgent(task);
```
Secure:
```
const submit = (tenantId: string, task: Task): Promise<Result> => {
  if (activeRuns(tenantId) >= TENANT_MAX) throw new Error("tenant full");
  return tenantLimiter(tenantId).schedule(() => runAgent(task));
};
```

Each tenant has a maximum number of in-flight runs and a spend ceiling, both checked at admission, and a request is rejected or queued once either cap is reached.

**Security Rule:** The output of one agent must be validated against an expected schema and acceptance criteria before it is consumed by a downstream agent, so a malformed or fabricated result is rejected at the handoff rather than propagated.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A downstream agent consumes the raw text or object emitted by an upstream agent with no schema check or acceptance test, so a hallucinated field, a wrong type, or an invented entity flows straight into the next stage of the chain.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Every agent-to-agent handoff passes through a validation gate that enforces a typed schema and content acceptance rules, and a failing output is rejected or routed back for correction before any downstream agent sees it.

```
agent_b.run(upstream_result)          # consumed unchecked
```
Secure:

```
upstream_result = agent_a.run(task)
if validate_schema(upstream_result):  # gate at the handoff
    agent_b.run(upstream_result)
```

```
await executorAgent.run(plan.steps);
```

```
await executorAgent.run(plan.steps);
```

```
executor_agent.invoke(plan["steps"])
```

```
executor_agent.invoke(plan.steps)     # Pydantic-validated handoff
```

```
await executorAgent.run(plan.steps);
```

```
await executorAgent.run(plan.steps);
```

**Security Rule:** Factual claims produced by an agent must be grounded against an authoritative source — a retrieval store, a database, or a tool result — before they are passed downstream or presented as fact.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An agent generates facts, identifiers, or values from the model's own parametric memory and the chain treats them as verified, so an invented citation, record id, or figure is amplified by every downstream agent that builds on it.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Claims that downstream agents will rely on are checked against a retrieval or database lookup, and any claim that cannot be grounded is dropped, flagged as unverified, or sent back for regeneration.

```

    report.append(answer)
```

```
await summaryAgent.run(claim);
```

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An agent commits its raw output directly into shared scratchpad or persistent memory, so every later agent and later run reads a fabricated value as authoritative context and the error compounds across the system.

**Security Rule:** The active objective must be checked for unauthorized change before each planning or tool step, by comparing it against an integrity-protected copy captured when the task was authorized.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The goal is held in mutable agent state and read freshly each turn with no check, so a single tampered turn silently replaces it and every later step serves the attacker's objective.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The authorized goal is hashed or signed at task start, stored separately from working memory, and re-verified before each step so any drift halts the run for review.

```

```

```

```

```

```

**Security Rule:** An agent must only be able to take actions on an explicit allowlist scoped to its current task, so a misaligned strategy cannot reach a tool or operation the operator never sanctioned.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The agent is handed the full tool registry regardless of its task, so when it pursues an unintended strategy it can call powerful tools such as deleting data or sending mail to make that strategy succeed.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The agent is given only the minimal set of tools its current task requires, and any call outside that allowlist is rejected before it executes.

```

const agent = new Agent({ tools: registry.list().filter(t => allowed.has(t.name)) });
```

**Security Rule:** An agent's report that it completed a task must be confirmed against an objective check before the result is accepted, so a fabricated or over-optimistic success claim cannot be trusted as fact.

**Security Rule:** An agent must be required to surface failures, partial results, and low confidence explicitly, so it cannot satisfy its objective by quietly papering over a problem and reporting unqualified success.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The agent's output schema only allows a success result, so when the agent cannot complete the task it fabricates a plausible answer rather than admitting the failure.

**Security Rule:** Agent actions and reasoning traces must be observed by a separate monitor that the agent cannot influence, so misaligned strategies are detected from outside rather than relying on the agent to report them.

**Security Rule:** When one agent reviews or approves another agent's work, the reviewer must be independent and adversarially oriented, so two agents cannot converge on a strategy that approves each other while failing the real objective.

Secure:
```

```

**Security Rule:** Agent audit logs must be written to append-only, tamper-evident storage so a compromised agent or operator cannot silently delete or rewrite the record of what was done.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Audit entries are written to a mutable file or table that the agent process itself can overwrite or truncate, so the trail can be altered after the fact to hide actions.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Audit events are appended to write-once storage or a hash-chained log, where each entry references the digest of the previous one, so any deletion or edit is detectable.

```

```

```
entry.prevHash = lastHash;

```

```

```


Audit events must be emitted as structured records with explicit fields rather than free-form text, so an incident responder can query and filter the agent's activity reliably.
Agent activity is recorded as interpolated human-readable strings, so the trail cannot be queried by tool, actor, or outcome and key fields are easily lost in formatting.
Each event is a structured object with named fields for event type, actor, tool, arguments, outcome, and timestamp, emitted to a sink that preserves the structure for later querying.

**Security Rule:** Every human-in-the-loop approval, rejection, or override of an agent action must be logged with the deciding user, the decision, the action it applied to, and the time, so accountability is preserved.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A human approves or overrides an agent's proposed action and the decision is acted on with no record, so later no one can show who authorized a sensitive operation.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The approval gate writes an audit event capturing the deciding user, the verdict, the target action id, and the timestamp before the agent is allowed to proceed.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if userApproved: agent.proceed(action)
```
Secure:
```
audit.log(event="approval", user=user.id, verdict=verdict, action=action.id)
if verdict == "approved": agent.proceed(action)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (approval.granted) await agent.resume(action);
```
Secure:
```
auditLog.record({ event: "hitl_decision", userId: approver.id,
                  verdict: approval.verdict, actionId: action.id });
if (approval.granted) await agent.resume(action);
```

Vulnerable:
```
if approval.granted:
    await agent.resume(action)
```
Secure:
```
audit_log.info("hitl_decision", user_id=approver.id,
               verdict=approval.verdict, action_id=action.id)
if approval.granted:
    await agent.resume(action)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (approval.granted) await agent.resume(action);
```
Secure:
```
auditLog.record({ event: "hitl_decision", userId: approver.id,
                  verdict: approval.verdict, actionId: action.id });
if (approval.granted) await agent.resume(action);
```

**Security Rule:** Audit events must carry a timezone-explicit timestamp from a trusted, synchronized clock so the ordering of actions across agents can be established without dispute.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Events are stamped with a naive local time, or with a value the agent itself supplies, so timelines across agents disagree and the real order of actions cannot be proven.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The audit layer applies an authoritative UTC timestamp at write time, independent of agent-supplied values, so all events share one consistent and comparable time base.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
entry.time = localTimeString()     # naive, agent-controlled
```
Secure:
```
entry.time = trustedClock.utcNow().isoformat()
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
entry.ts = new Date().toString();
```
Secure:
```
entry.ts = new Date().toISOString();
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
entry["ts"] = datetime.now().isoformat()
```
Secure:
```
entry["ts"] = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
entry.ts = new Date().toString();
```
Secure:
```
entry.ts = new Date().toISOString();
```

The audit layer applies an authoritative UTC timestamp at write time, independent of agent-supplied values, so all events share one consistent and comparable time base.

**Security Rule:** Audit logging of an agent action must not be bypassed when the action throws, times out, or is cancelled — failed and partial attempts must be recorded just like successful ones.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The audit write is placed only on the success path, so an action that fails midway leaves no trace and an attacker can cause errors to suppress the record of an attempt.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The audit event is emitted in a finally block or equivalent guaranteed path, recording the outcome including error and cancellation, so every attempt is accounted for.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
result = tool.run(args); audit.log(result)   # skipped if run throws
```
Secure:
```
try: result = tool.run(args); finally: audit.log(outcome_of(result))
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const result = await tool.run(args);
auditLog.record({ event: "tool_done", outcome: "ok" });
```
Secure:
```
let outcome = "error";
try { const result = await tool.run(args); outcome = "ok"; }
finally { auditLog.record({ event: "tool_done", outcome }); }
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
result = await tool.run(args)
audit_log.info("tool_done", outcome="ok")
```
Secure:
```
try:
    result = await tool.run(args); outcome = "ok"
finally:
    audit_log.info("tool_done", outcome=outcome)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const result = await tool.run(args);
auditLog.record({ event: "tool_done", outcome: "ok" });
```
Secure:
```
let outcome = "error";
try { const result = await tool.run(args); outcome = "ok"; }
finally { auditLog.record({ event: "tool_done", outcome }); }
```

The audit write is placed only on the success path, so an action that fails midway leaves no trace and an attacker can cause errors to suppress the record of an attempt.
The audit event is emitted in a finally block or equivalent guaranteed path, recording the outcome including error and cancellation, so every attempt is accounted for.

**Security Rule:** Sensitive values in audit records must be redacted or hashed rather than dropped, so logs stay safe to retain while still proving which inputs an agent acted on.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** To avoid storing secrets, whole arguments or payloads are omitted from the audit record, so the trail no longer shows what the agent actually operated on and the action cannot be verified.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Sensitive fields are masked or replaced with a stable salted hash while non-sensitive structure is retained, so the record stays both privacy-safe and forensically meaningful.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
audit.log(tool=name)               # args dropped entirely
```
Secure:
```
audit.log(tool=name, args=redactSecrets(args))
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
auditLog.record({ event: "tool_call", tool: name });
```
Secure:
```
auditLog.record({ event: "tool_call", tool: name, args: redact(args) });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
audit_log.info("tool_call", tool=name)
```
Secure:
```
audit_log.info("tool_call", tool=name, args=mask_sensitive(kwargs))
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
auditLog.record({ event: "tool_call", tool: name });
```
Secure:
```
auditLog.record({ event: "tool_call", tool: name, args: redact(args) });
```

Sensitive values in audit records must be redacted or hashed rather than dropped, so logs stay safe to retain while still proving which inputs an agent acted on.
To avoid storing secrets, whole arguments or payloads are omitted from the audit record, so the trail no longer shows what the agent actually operated on and the action cannot be verified.
Sensitive fields are masked or replaced with a stable salted hash while non-sensitive structure is retained, so the record stays both privacy-safe and forensically meaningful.

Every message received from another agent must carry a verifiable proof of origin, and the receiving agent must authenticate that proof before acting on the message rather than trusting a self-declared sender field.
A receiving agent reads a plain sender or from field out of the message body and trusts it, so any agent that can place a message on the bus can claim to be a privileged peer.
Each message is signed by the sending agent with its private key, and the receiver verifies the signature against the sender's registered public key before the message is processed.

**Security Rule:** An agent connecting to a tool, model endpoint, or peer agent must verify the remote identity, and the remote side must verify the agent, so neither end can be silently swapped for an impostor.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An agent opens a connection to a service by hostname alone and presents no client credential, so a spoofed endpoint accepts the traffic and a malicious caller is treated as a trusted agent.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The connection uses mutual TLS or an equivalent two-way credential exchange, so the agent validates the service certificate and the service validates the agent certificate before any payload flows.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
conn = connect(service_host)          # no server identity check, no client cert
```
Secure:
```
conn = connect(service_host, verify_server_cert=true, client_cert=agent_cert)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const res = await fetch(toolUrl, { method: "POST", body });
```
Secure:
```
const agent = new https.Agent({ ca, cert: agentCert, key: agentKey });
const res = await fetch(toolUrl, { method: "POST", body, agent });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
resp = httpx.post(tool_url, json=payload, verify=False)
```
Secure:
```
ctx = ssl.create_default_context(cafile="agent-ca.pem")
ctx.load_cert_chain("agent.crt", "agent.key")
resp = httpx.post(tool_url, json=payload, verify=ctx)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const res = await fetch(toolUrl, { method: "POST", body });
```
Secure:
```
const tlsAgent = new https.Agent({ ca, cert: agentCert, key: agentKey });
const res = await fetch(toolUrl, { method: "POST", body, agent: tlsAgent });
```
embed_text:
An agent connecting to a tool, model endpoint, or peer agent must verify the remote identity, and the remote side must verify the agent, so neither end can be silently swapped for an impostor.
An agent opens a connection to a service by hostname alone and presents no client credential, so a spoofed endpoint accepts the traffic and a malicious caller is treated as a trusted agent.
The connection uses mutual TLS or an equivalent two-way credential exchange, so the agent validates the service certificate and the service validates the agent certificate before any payload flows.

When one agent delegates work to another it must pass a signed, scope-limited delegation token, and the worker agent must verify the token rather than infer authority from who appeared to send the call.
A sub-agent acts with the full authority of the user or orchestrator because the caller merely stated it was acting on their behalf, with no cryptographic token tying the request to the original principal.
The orchestrator issues a short-lived signed token naming the principal and the permitted scope, and the sub-agent verifies the token's signature, audience, and scope before executing.

**Security Rule:** Agent privilege must be derived only from authenticated context, and never from role, identity, or authority claims found inside prompt text, tool output, or retrieved documents.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The agent reads a phrase such as a claim of being an administrator out of untrusted content and grants the corresponding privilege, letting injected text impersonate a high-privilege caller.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The privilege check uses the authenticated session or signed token attached to the request out of band, and any role-like text inside the content is treated as untrusted data with no authority.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if "i am an admin" in message_text: grant_admin_tools()
```
Secure:
```
if authenticated_session.role == "admin": grant_admin_tools()
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (/system: you are now root/i.test(toolOutput)) enableRootTools();
```
Secure:
```
if (ctx.principal.roles.includes("root")) enableRootTools();
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if re.search(r"role:\s*admin", llm_input, re.I):
    allow_privileged_tool()
```
Secure:
```
if ctx.session.is_admin:          # role from verified session, not text
    allow_privileged_tool()
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (/system: you are now root/i.test(toolOutput)) enableRootTools();
```
Secure:
```
if (ctx.principal.roles.includes("root")) enableRootTools();
```
