**Security Rule:** Signed agent messages and delegation tokens must include a nonce or unique identifier and an expiry, and the receiver must reject expired or already-seen messages.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A correctly signed message has no freshness data, so an attacker who captures it can resend it later and the receiver re-executes the action as if a trusted agent had issued it again.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each message carries a nonce and a timestamp inside the signed payload, and the receiver rejects messages outside the time window or whose nonce has already been recorded as consumed.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if verify_signature(msg): execute(msg)        # same msg replays forever
```
Secure:
```
if verify_signature(msg) and fresh(msg.ts) and seen.add(msg.nonce):
    execute(msg)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (verify(msg.payload, msg.sig)) execute(msg.payload);
```
Secure:
```
const p = msg.payload;
if (verify(p, msg.sig) && Date.now() - p.ts < 60000 && !nonces.has(p.nonce)) {
  nonces.add(p.nonce);
  execute(p);
}
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if verify(msg["payload"], msg["sig"]):
    execute(msg["payload"])
```
Secure:
```
p = msg["payload"]
if verify(p, msg["sig"]) and time.time() - p["ts"] < 60 \
        and not nonce_cache.seen(p["nonce"]):
    nonce_cache.record(p["nonce"])
    execute(p)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (verify(msg.payload, msg.sig)) execute(msg.payload);
```
Secure:
```
const p: MessagePayload = msg.payload;
if (verify(p, msg.sig) && Date.now() - p.ts < 60_000 && !nonces.has(p.nonce)) {
  nonces.add(p.nonce);
  execute(p);
}
```
embed_text:
Signed agent messages and delegation tokens must include a nonce or unique identifier and an expiry, and the receiver must reject expired or already-seen messages.
A correctly signed message has no freshness data, so an attacker who captures it can resend it later and the receiver re-executes the action as if a trusted agent had issued it again.
Each message carries a nonce and a timestamp inside the signed payload, and the receiver rejects messages outside the time window or whose nonce has already been recorded as consumed.

**Security Rule:** Agent identities and their public keys must be resolved from a trusted registry or directory, never from key material or endpoints advertised inside an incoming message.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The receiver verifies a message using a public key that the message itself supplied, so an attacker simply ships their own key and the signature check passes for a forged identity.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The receiver looks the claimed agent name up in a trusted registry to obtain the pinned public key, then verifies the signature against that key, ignoring any key embedded in the message.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
verify(msg.payload, msg.sig, key=msg.public_key)   # key from the message
```
Secure:
```
key = registry.public_key_for(msg.claimed_agent_id)
verify(msg.payload, msg.sig, key=key)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const ok = verify(msg.payload, msg.sig, importKey(msg.senderPubKey));
```
Secure:
```
const key = await agentRegistry.getKey(msg.agentId);
const ok = verify(msg.payload, msg.sig, key);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
pubkey = load_key(msg["sender_pubkey"])
ok = verify(msg["payload"], msg["sig"], pubkey)
```
Secure:
```
pubkey = agent_registry.get_key(msg["agent_id"])    # trusted directory
ok = verify(msg["payload"], msg["sig"], pubkey)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const ok = verify(msg.payload, msg.sig, importKey(msg.senderPubKey));
```
Secure:
```
const key = await agentRegistry.getKey(msg.agentId);
const ok = verify(msg.payload, msg.sig, key);
```

The receiver verifies a message using a public key that the message itself supplied, so an attacker simply ships their own key and the signature check passes for a forged identity.
The receiver looks the claimed agent name up in a trusted registry to obtain the pinned public key, then verifies the signature against that key, ignoring any key embedded in the message.

**Security Rule:** Each agent must run under its own distinct service identity and credential, so actions are attributable to a single agent and one agent cannot transparently act as another.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Every agent in the system authenticates with the same shared API key or service account, so any agent can impersonate any other and the audit log cannot tell which agent acted.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each agent is provisioned a unique identity with its own credential and least-privilege scope, so calls are attributable and a compromised agent cannot present as a different one.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client = ApiClient(api_key=SHARED_AGENT_KEY)   # all agents share it
```
Secure:
```
client = ApiClient(credential=identity_for(this_agent_id))
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const client = new ToolClient({ token: process.env.SHARED_AGENT_TOKEN });
```
Secure:
```
const client = new ToolClient({ token: await vault.tokenFor(agentId) });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
def make_client():
    return ToolClient(token=os.environ["SHARED_AGENT_TOKEN"])
```
Secure:
```
def make_client(agent_id):
    return ToolClient(token=secrets_manager.token_for(agent_id))
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const client = new ToolClient({ token: process.env.SHARED_AGENT_TOKEN });
```
Secure:
```
const client = new ToolClient({ token: await vault.tokenFor(agentId) });
```

Every agent in the system authenticates with the same shared API key or service account, so any agent can impersonate any other and the audit log cannot tell which agent acted.
Each agent is provisioned a unique identity with its own credential and least-privilege scope, so calls are attributable and a compromised agent cannot present as a different one.

The identity of the originating human principal must be carried, unchanged and verifiable, through every agent hop, so a downstream agent cannot substitute or elevate the acting user.
An intermediate agent re-issues a downstream request under its own identity or under a user value it chose, so the original principal is lost and the agent silently impersonates a different user.
The verified principal travels in a signed chain where each hop adds its own attestation without rewriting the subject, so the final service can confirm both the principal and the full delegation path.

Route agent actions to a human reviewer based on assessed risk, so low-risk reversible actions proceed automatically and a human only sees the high-risk irreversible decisions that genuinely need judgement.
Every agent action triggers a human approval prompt regardless of its risk, so the reviewer is buried in trivial confirmations and loses the attention budget needed to scrutinise the dangerous ones.
Each action is scored for risk and reversibility, and only actions above a defined threshold are escalated to a human while the rest run under automated policy with an audit trail.

document:
**Security Rule:** Bound the number of approval requests a single human reviewer receives per time window, and have the agent slow down or queue work when that bound is reached rather than firing requests unthrottled.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An agent or fleet of agents emits approval requests with no rate limit, so a reviewer faces a burst far larger than they can assess and falls back to bulk-clicking to clear the backlog.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Approval requests pass through a rate limiter scoped to the reviewer, and excess requests are queued or deferred so the human always faces a sustainable, reviewable arrival rate.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
send_approval_request(reviewer, action)   # unbounded firehose
```
Secure:
```
if approval_budget.allow(reviewer):
    send_approval_request(reviewer, action)
else:
    queue_for_later(reviewer, action)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
actions.forEach(a => pushApproval(reviewer, a));
```
Secure:
```
for (const a of actions) {
  await reviewerLimiter.take(reviewer);   // throttled arrival
  pushApproval(reviewer, a);
}
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
for finding in findings:
    notify_reviewer(reviewer, finding)
```
Secure:
```
limiter = RateLimiter(max_per_hour=20, scope=reviewer)
for finding in findings:
    await limiter.acquire()
    notify_reviewer(reviewer, finding)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const notify = (a: Action) => queue.publish(reviewer, a);
```
Secure:
```
const notify = async (a: Action) => {
  if (await limiter.tryAcquire(reviewer)) queue.publish(reviewer, a);
  else deferred.push(a);
};
```
embed_text:
Bound the number of approval requests a single human reviewer receives per time window, and have the agent slow down or queue work when that bound is reached rather than firing requests unthrottled.
An agent or fleet of agents emits approval requests with no rate limit, so a reviewer faces a burst far larger than they can assess and falls back to bulk-clicking to clear the backlog.
Approval requests pass through a rate limiter scoped to the reviewer, and excess requests are queued or deferred so the human always faces a sustainable, reviewable arrival rate.

When a reviewer approves a batch of agent actions, treat the approval as covering only the items actually shown, and require a fresh decision for any item added, changed, or reordered after the batch was presented.
A single approve action is applied to a whole batch including items that were appended or mutated after the reviewer saw the list, so actions the human never inspected execute under a borrowed approval.
Each batch is hashed or fingerprinted at presentation time, and the approval is validated against that fingerprint so any later change invalidates it and forces re-review of the affected items.

**Security Rule:** Present each approval request with a concise, decision-focused summary of what the action does, why it was proposed, and what its blast radius is, so the reviewer can decide without wading through raw agent output.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Approval prompts dump raw tool calls, full diffs, or unstructured agent reasoning, so the cognitive cost of each review is high and the reviewer skims or approves blindly to keep up.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each request carries a structured summary — action, target, reversibility, and risk rationale — with full detail available on demand, so the common case is fast and accurate.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
show_to_reviewer(raw_tool_call_json)
```
Secure:
```
show_to_reviewer(summarize(action))   # what / target / risk / reversible
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
renderApproval(JSON.stringify(toolCall));
```
Secure:
```
renderApproval({ does: a.summary, affects: a.target,
                 risk: a.riskTier, undo: a.reversible });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
prompt = json.dumps(agent_state)      # whole state, no framing
ask(reviewer, prompt)
```
Secure:
```
card = ReviewCard(action=a.name, target=a.target,
                   risk=a.risk_tier, reversible=a.reversible)
ask(reviewer, card)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
function present(a: Action) { return rawDump(a); }
```
Secure:
```
function present(a: Action): ReviewCard {
  return { summary: a.summary, blastRadius: a.target, risk: a.riskTier };
}
```

Approval prompts dump raw tool calls, full diffs, or unstructured agent reasoning, so the cognitive cost of each review is high and the reviewer skims or approves blindly to keep up.
Each request carries a structured summary — action, target, reversibility, and risk rationale — with full detail available on demand, so the common case is fast and accurate.

**Security Rule:** When a reviewer does not respond to an approval request within the allotted window, treat the action as denied or held, never as approved, so an overwhelmed or absent human cannot become a silent yes.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An approval request that times out is treated as an implicit approval, so flooding a reviewer until requests expire unreviewed becomes a reliable way to push actions through.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A timed-out or unanswered request resolves to denied or parked, requiring an explicit affirmative human decision before the action can ever proceed.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
decision = wait_for_human(req, timeout=300) or APPROVE   # timeout = yes
```
Secure:
```
decision = wait_for_human(req, timeout=300) or DENY      # timeout = no
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

```

embed_text:
When a reviewer does not respond to an approval request within the allotted window, treat the action as denied or held, never as approved, so an overwhelmed or absent human cannot become a silent yes.
An approval request that times out is treated as an implicit approval, so flooding a reviewer until requests expire unreviewed becomes a reliable way to push actions through.
A timed-out or unanswered request resolves to denied or parked, requiring an explicit affirmative human decision before the action can ever proceed.

**Security Rule:** Monitor reviewer behaviour for signs of rubber-stamping — near-zero dwell time, uniform approve rates, bursty bulk clicks — and surface or throttle when a reviewer is no longer meaningfully evaluating requests.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Approval decisions are recorded without any signal of how the human reached them, so a reviewer who approves every item in milliseconds is indistinguishable from one who carefully evaluated each one.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each decision captures review-effort signals such as dwell time and the approve-versus-reject mix, and anomalously fast or uniform patterns trigger an alert or a forced slow-down.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
record_decision(reviewer, action, "approved")
```
Secure:
```
record_decision(reviewer, action, "approved", dwell_ms=elapsed)
if rubber_stamp_detector.flag(reviewer):
    require_justification(reviewer)
```

Vulnerable:
```
saveDecision({ reviewer, action, verdict: "approved" });
```
Secure:

```
saveDecision({ reviewer, action, verdict: "approved", dwellMs });
if (dwellStats(reviewer).p50 < MIN_REVIEW_MS) raiseAnomaly(reviewer);
```

```

if metrics.median_dwell(reviewer) < MIN_REVIEW_MS:
    alert_security("possible rubber-stamping", reviewer)
```

```

};
```

**Security Rule:** Deduplicate identical approval requests and coalesce closely related ones into a single reviewable decision, so a human is never asked to judge the same thing many times or to piece together a scattered set of related actions.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Retrying agents or parallel sub-agents each raise their own approval request for the same or related action, so the reviewer is flooded with redundant prompts and loses the thread of what is being asked.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Requests are keyed by a stable action identity, so duplicates merge into one prompt and related actions are grouped under a single decision with shared context.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
for agent in agents:
    send_approval_request(agent.proposed_action)   # N copies
```
Secure:
```
pending = {}
for agent in agents:
    pending[action_key(agent.proposed_action)] = agent.proposed_action
for action in pending.values():
    send_approval_request(action)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
proposals.forEach(p => requestApproval(p));
```
Secure:
```
const byKey = new Map(proposals.map(p => [actionKey(p), p]));
byKey.forEach(p => requestApproval(p));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
async def on_propose(action):
    await request_approval(action)        # fires per retry
```
Secure:
```
seen: set[str] = set()
async def on_propose(action):
    key = action_fingerprint(action)
    if key in seen: return
    seen.add(key)
    await request_approval(action)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
function onProposal(p: Proposal) { requestApproval(p); }
```
Secure:
```
const seen = new Set<string>();
function onProposal(p: Proposal) {
  const k = fingerprint(p);
  if (!seen.has(k)) { seen.add(k); requestApproval(p); }
}
```

Retrying agents or parallel sub-agents each raise their own approval request for the same or related action, so the reviewer is flooded with redundant prompts and loses the thread of what is being asked.
Requests are keyed by a stable action identity, so duplicates merge into one prompt and related actions are grouped under a single decision with shared context.

**Security Rule:** Cap the number of approval requests outstanding against a single reviewer at once, and pause the agent that wants to add more until the reviewer has cleared the queue, applying backpressure to the workflow.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An agent continues generating work and approval requests while the reviewer's queue grows without bound, so the human faces an ever-deeper backlog they can only clear by skipping review.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A bounded semaphore or queue limit gates new approval requests, so the agent blocks and slows its own progress until the reviewer has capacity, keeping the queue depth reviewable.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
while plan.has_next():
    request_approval(plan.next())     # queue grows unbounded
```
Secure:
```
while plan.has_next():
    await approval_slots.acquire()    # blocks past MAX_OUTSTANDING
    request_approval(plan.next())
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
for (const a of plan) enqueueReview(a);
```
Secure:
```
for (const a of plan) {
  await approvalSemaphore.acquire();   // backpressure on the agent
  enqueueReview(a).finally(() => approvalSemaphore.release());
}
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
for action in plan:
    await enqueue_for_review(action)
```
Secure:
```
sem = asyncio.Semaphore(MAX_OUTSTANDING_APPROVALS)
async def submit(action):
    async with sem:
        await enqueue_for_review(action)
        await wait_decision(action)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
plan.forEach(a => reviewQueue.push(a));
```
Secure:
```
async function submit(a: Action): Promise<void> {
  await pool.withSlot(async () => {       // pool size = MAX_OUTSTANDING
    await reviewQueue.pushAndAwait(a);
  });
}
```

An agent continues generating work and approval requests while the reviewer's queue grows without bound, so the human faces an ever-deeper backlog they can only clear by skipping review.
A bounded semaphore or queue limit gates new approval requests, so the agent blocks and slows its own progress until the reviewer has capacity, keeping the queue depth reviewable.

**Security Rule:** Distribute approval requests across a pool of qualified reviewers and escalate when any individual is at capacity, so no single human becomes the bottleneck that is forced to rubber-stamp under load.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** All approval requests are routed to one named reviewer or a single shared inbox, so that person saturates under load and approval quality collapses with no fallback.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Requests are assigned by load-aware routing across an on-call pool, and a reviewer at capacity causes overflow to a peer or a supervisor rather than a deeper personal backlog.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
assign_review(action, reviewer="alice")    # single point of overload
```
Secure:
```
assign_review(action, reviewer=pool.least_loaded_available())
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
routeApproval(action, "ops-inbox");
```
Secure:
```
const r = reviewerPool.leastLoaded(action.domain) ?? onCallBackup();
routeApproval(action, r);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
DEFAULT_REVIEWER = "security-lead"
notify(DEFAULT_REVIEWER, action)
```
Secure:
```
reviewer = reviewer_pool.pick(skill=action.domain)
if reviewer is None:
    reviewer = escalate_to_supervisor(action)
notify(reviewer, action)
```

[[TYPESCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const reviewerFor = (_: Action): string => "alice";
```
Secure:
```
const reviewerFor = (a: Action): Reviewer =>
  pool.assign(a) ?? pool.overflowTo(a.domain);
```

Distribute approval requests across a pool of qualified reviewers and escalate when any individual is at capacity, so no single human becomes the bottleneck that is forced to rubber-stamp under load.
All approval requests are routed to one named reviewer or a single shared inbox, so that person saturates under load and approval quality collapses with no fallback.
Requests are assigned by load-aware routing across an on-call pool, and a reviewer at capacity causes overflow to a peer or a supervisor rather than a deeper personal backlog.

Require deliberate, high-friction confirmation — a typed phrase, a second approver, or a cooling-off delay — for irreversible or high-blast-radius actions, so they cannot be cleared by the same reflexive click used for routine items.
Destructive or irreversible actions use the identical one-click approve control as routine ones, so a reviewer in firehose mode approves a catastrophic action by reflex before registering what it was.
High-impact actions demand explicit friction such as typed confirmation, dual control, or a mandatory delay, breaking the reflexive approval pattern and forcing conscious attention.
